content.text: Parallel Execution in LangGraph
Parallel execution runs multiple independent nodes simultaneously and waits for all of them to complete before proceeding. The key property: wall-clock time equals the duration of the
slowest branch
, not the sum of all branches. In a four-branch review where each takes 0.8 seconds, parallel execution completes in 0.8 seconds instead of 3.2 seconds.

attrs.alt: Parallel execution diagram: fan_out node fans into four parallel branches, all converging at a merge node

content.text: Fan-Out with Multiple Edges

The simplest parallel pattern adds edges from the entry point to each parallel node and edges from each parallel node to a shared merge point. LangGraph detects that the merge node has multiple incoming edges and waits for all of them before executing it.

from typing import TypedDict, Annotated
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
import operator
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser

llm = ChatOpenAI(model="claude-sonnet-5", temperature=0)
parser = StrOutputParser()

class ReviewState(TypedDict):
    document: str
    # Annotated with operator.add so each parallel branch appends its result
    reviews: Annotated[list[str], operator.add]

def review_clarity(state: ReviewState) -> ReviewState:
    chain = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template("Review CLARITY of this document in one sentence: {document}") | llm | parser
    return {"reviews": [f"Clarity: {chain.invoke(state)}"]}

def review_accuracy(state: ReviewState) -> ReviewState:
    chain = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template("Review ACCURACY of this document in one sentence: {document}") | llm | parser
    return {"reviews": [f"Accuracy: {chain.invoke(state)}"]}

def review_completeness(state: ReviewState) -> ReviewState:
    chain = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template("Review COMPLETENESS of this document in one sentence: {document}") | llm | parser
    return {"reviews": [f"Completeness: {chain.invoke(state)}"]}

def merge_reviews(state: ReviewState) -> ReviewState:
    # All three reviews are already in state["reviews"] by the time this runs
    combined = "\n".join(state["reviews"])
    print("All reviews received:", combined[:120])
    return {}  # nothing new to write; reviews are already in state

graph = StateGraph(ReviewState)
graph.add_node("review_clarity", review_clarity)
graph.add_node("review_accuracy", review_accuracy)
graph.add_node("review_completeness", review_completeness)
graph.add_node("merge_reviews", merge_reviews)

# Fan-out: START -> all three review nodes simultaneously
graph.set_entry_point("review_clarity")  # first node
# add_edge from each parallel node to the merge node (fan-in)
for name in ["review_clarity", "review_accuracy", "review_completeness"]:
    graph.add_edge(name, "merge_reviews")
graph.add_edge("merge_reviews", END)

chain = graph.compile()
result = chain.invoke({"document": "Annual financial report 2026.", "reviews": []})
print(f"Received {len(result['reviews'])} reviews")
The
Annotated[list[str], operator.add]
reducer on
reviews
is what makes fan-in work. Each parallel branch returns
. Without the reducer, the last branch to complete would overwrite the others. With
operator.add
, LangGraph concatenates all three lists so the merge node receives all reviews at once.
Dynamic Fan-Out with Send

When the number of parallel branches is determined at runtime — processing one section per document section, one query per search term — use the Send API. Each Send() call spawns an independent branch with its own input dict.

from typing import TypedDict, Annotated
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
from langgraph.constants import Send
import operator
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser

llm = ChatOpenAI(model="claude-sonnet-5", temperature=0)
parser = StrOutputParser()

class AnalysisState(TypedDict):
    document: str
    sections: list[str]           # populated by split_document
    analyses: Annotated[list[str], operator.add]  # each section adds one entry
    final_report: str

def split_document(state: AnalysisState) -> AnalysisState:
    # Split document into sections for parallel analysis
    raw = state["document"]
    # Simplified: split on double newlines
    sections = [s.strip() for s in raw.split("\n\n") if s.strip()]
    return {"sections": sections}

def fan_out_sections(state: AnalysisState):
    # Send() spawns one parallel branch per section
    return [Send("analyse_section", {"document": state["document"], "section": s, "sections": state["sections"], "analyses": [], "final_report": ""})
            for s in state["sections"]]

def analyse_section(state: dict) -> dict:
    chain = ChatPromptTemplate.from_template("Summarise this section in one sentence: {section}") | llm | parser
    return {"analyses": [chain.invoke(state)]}

def compile_report(state: AnalysisState) -> AnalysisState:
    report = "\n".join(f"- {a}" for a in state["analyses"])
    return {"final_report": report}

graph = StateGraph(AnalysisState)
graph.add_node("split_document", split_document)
graph.add_node("analyse_section", analyse_section)
graph.add_node("compile_report", compile_report)

graph.set_entry_point("split_document")
graph.add_conditional_edges("split_document", fan_out_sections, ["analyse_section"])
graph.add_edge("analyse_section", "compile_report")
graph.add_edge("compile_report", END)

chain = graph.compile()
result = chain.invoke({
    "document": "Section A: financials.\n\nSection B: operations.\n\nSection C: outlook.",
    "sections": [], "analyses": [], "final_report": "",
})
print(result["final_report"])
Send() is LangGraph's mechanism for dynamic parallelism. The router function returns a list of
Send

objects, one per item. Each branch executes the named node with the provided state dict. The branch's output is merged back into the main graph's state via the Annotated reducer. Use Send() when the fan-out count is known only after a preceding step runs.
Async Parallelism with asyncio.gather

For parallel LLM calls that do not need LangGraph's state management, asyncio.gather() is simpler and equally effective. Each coroutine is a chain.ainvoke() call; all run concurrently in the event loop.

import asyncio
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.prompts import ChatPromptTemplate
from langchain_core.output_parsers import StrOutputParser

llm = ChatOpenAI(model="claude-sonnet-5", temperature=0)
parser = StrOutputParser()

async def analyse_dimension(document: str, dimension: str) -> str:
    chain = (
        ChatPromptTemplate.from_template("Analyse the {dimension} of: {document}")
        | llm | parser
    )
    return await chain.ainvoke({"dimension": dimension, "document": document})

async def parallel_review(document: str) -> dict[str, str]:
    dimensions = ["clarity", "accuracy", "completeness", "tone"]
    # All four run concurrently; total latency = slowest single dimension
    results = await asyncio.gather(*[
        analyse_dimension(document, d) for d in dimensions
    ])
    return dict(zip(dimensions, results))

# Run
import asyncio
reviews = asyncio.run(parallel_review("Annual report 2026: revenue grew 15%."))
for dim, review in reviews.items():
    print(f"{dim}: {review[:60]}")
asyncio.gather()

is the right tool when: the calls are truly independent (no shared state to merge), you are already in an async context, and you do not need checkpointing or retries at the graph level. Use LangGraph's parallel edges when you need state persistence, conditional routing after the merge, or the fan-in logic to be explicit in the graph topology.
Structured Output from Parallel Branches
When each branch returns a structured Pydantic object, the merge node receives a list of typed objects that are easy to aggregate.
Each scorer uses
with_structured_output(SectionScore)
so the merge step receives strongly-typed objects rather than free-form text. The
weighted_score
computation in the merge node is pure Python — no model call needed, because the structured outputs already contain exactly the numeric scores required.
When to Use Parallel vs Sequential
Use parallel when
Use sequential when
Branches are independent (no data dependency)
Step B needs step A's output
Latency is a constraint
Correctness requires a fixed order
Multiple reviewers or evaluators on the same input
One reviewer builds on the previous review
Fan-out count is fixed or dynamically known
The pipeline is a single linear chain
Model API rate limits allow concurrent calls
Rate limits force serialisation

Parallel execution is not always faster. If an LLM provider enforces per-minute token limits, firing four requests simultaneously may cause throttling that makes total latency worse than sequential execution. Monitor rate-limit errors in production and tune concurrency accordingly.
Testing Parallel Branches
Parallel execution introduces a testing concern that sequential chains do not have: the order in which branches complete is non-deterministic. Tests must assert on set membership, not on list order.
Assert count, not order.
Use
sorted(result['results'])
or
set(result['results'])
when the list order from parallel branches is non-deterministic.
Test the reducer in isolation.
The Annotated reducer is the most failure-prone part of a fan-in. Test that two branches' outputs merge correctly before testing the full graph.
Test with a single branch.
Degenerate parallelism (one branch) should behave identically to sequential. If it does not, the reducer or merge node has a bug.
Summary

Parallel execution runs independent branches simultaneously, with wall-clock time equal to the slowest branch. LangGraph implements fan-out via multiple edges from one node and fan-in via Annotated reducers that merge each branch's updates. Send() enables dynamic fan-out when the branch count is unknown at graph-compile time. asyncio.gather() is the lighter alternative when state management is not needed. Always use Annotated reducers for fields that parallel branches write to, and assert on sorted results rather than ordered lists in tests.
